1: Chapter 9 provides the structural conclusion of the prologue (chapters 1–9) by presenting the two cosmic invitations in parallel: Wisdom's banquet (Proverbs 9:1–6) and Folly's doorstep (Proverbs 9:13–18), with intervening instructions (Proverbs 9:7–12). "Wisdom has built her house; she has hewn out her seven pillars" — the house-building image echoes Proverbs 14:1 (the wise woman who builds her house). "Seven pillars" (ʿammudêhā šibʿâ): the number seven is the number of completeness in biblical and ancient Near Eastern symbolism (seven days of creation; seven-branched menorah; seven as the cosmic total). The pillars may represent the complete and well-ordered household; some interpreters see the seven pillars as the seven books of wisdom (Solomonic texts), but the text does not specify. The spaciousness of the seven-pillared house signals Wisdom's abundance and hospitality.
2: "She has slaughtered her animals, mixed her wine, and set her table" — the three acts of banquet preparation (slaughter, wine-mixing, table-setting) follow the pattern of the ancient Near Eastern royal feast. "Mixed her wine" (māzəgâ yênāh) — wine in antiquity was typically mixed with water (and sometimes spices) before drinking; to serve unmixed wine was the exception for special occasions (Song of Solomon 7:3; Isaiah 25:6). The deliberate preparation contrasts with the haste and urgency of the Strange Woman's offer (Proverbs 7:14–18); Wisdom's hospitality is unhurried, complete, and generous. The feast evokes the messianic banquet tradition (Isaiah 25:6–9).
3: "She has sent out her servants" (šāləḥâ naʿărōtêhā) — Wisdom's servants are the sages, teachers, and parents who transmit her instruction in the community. "Calling from the heights of the city" — the elevated public proclamation of Proverbs 8:1–4 continues; Wisdom does not simply prepare the feast but actively recruits guests. The sending of servants parallels the royal parable tradition (cf. the parable of the great banquet in Luke 14:16–23 where servants are sent out to invite guests); the invitation is urgent and specific.
4: "Let the simple come in here" (mî-pétî yāsur hennâ, whoever is simple, turn here!) — the invitation targets pétî (the simple, naive, open person) as the primary audience; the teachable are those most likely to respond. "To the one who lacks understanding (ḥăsar-lēb, lacking heart/mind) she says" — the formula repeats in Proverbs 9:16 (Folly's identical words to the same audience). The structural parallel is deliberate: both Wisdom and Folly call to the same person with nearly the same words. The difference lies in what each offers and where each leads, not in the form of the invitation.
5: "Come and eat my bread; drink the wine I have mixed" — the bread (laḥmî, my bread) and wine (yênî māzagtî, the wine I have mixed) are both possessives: Wisdom offers what belongs to her. In the ancient world, sharing bread and wine was the most fundamental act of covenant community (cf. Exodus 24:11; Genesis 14:18). Wisdom's feast is a covenant meal, not merely a dinner party; eating with Wisdom is entering her household, her order, her community. The echo of the Passover meal and later Eucharistic imagery is not accidental in the tradition's reception history.
6: "Leave your simple ways and live (ʿizbu pətāʾyîm wihyû)" — the command to "leave" (ʿāzab, to abandon, forsake) naive simplicity uses the same verb for abandoning all sorts of things, including God (Jeremiah 1:16). Life (ḥayyû, live!) is the direct result of leaving simplicity — wisdom-choice is literally life-choice. "Walk in the path of understanding" — ʾašrû bəderek bînâ (make straight your way in the path of understanding) — the spatial metaphor of the two paths reaches its climactic invitation: this is the path, walk in it (cf. Isaiah 30:21). The prologue's entire argument is summarized in one imperative.
7: Verses 7–12 are often identified as an independent wisdom section inserted between the two banquet scenes. "Whoever rebukes a mocker earns insults" — the practical sociology of wisdom: the lēṣ (mocker/scoffer) is constitutionally closed to correction; rebuking him produces social blowback for the rebuker. "Whoever corrects a wicked man gets hurt" (rāšāʿ) — the wicked person's response to correction is violence. This is not defeatism but realistic social intelligence: knowing when rebuke is possible and when it is futile is itself a form of wisdom. Ancient wisdom traditions (Egyptian; Mesopotamian) similarly counsel discernment about which ears will hear.
8: "Do not rebuke a mocker or he will only hate you" — the negative consequence of rebuking the unreachable is relational: hatred. "Rebuke a wise person and he will love you for it" — the wise person's response to correction is the opposite: ʾōhăbeka (he will love you). The asymmetry defines the two categories: the scoffer and the wise are distinguished by their response to rebuke. This is wisdom's character-diagnosis: how a person responds to being told they are wrong is the most reliable indicator of their inner formation. The wise embrace correction; the foolish reject it violently.
9: "Teach the wise and they grow wiser still; instruct the righteous and they gain more" — the paradox of wisdom's education: those who already have wisdom receive more when taught; those who have nothing receive nothing from instruction. This is not favoritism but the nature of formative development — only those with the disposition to receive can actually absorb what is given. Ancient education in Israel (and Egypt and Mesopotamia) was selective and cumulative; the scribe's training built on prior formation. Wisdom cannot be poured into an unprepared vessel.
10: "The fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom" — the prologue's foundational thesis, stated first in Proverbs 1:7, is restated here as the conclusion of the prologue. ʾăḥēlat ḥokmâ — ʾāḥēlet is the beginning or primary part; in the ancient world this was a qualitative designation (the most fundamental part) as much as a temporal one. "Knowing the Holy One is understanding" (daʿat qədōšîm bînâ) — qədōšîm (Holy One, or Holy Ones — the plural could be a plural of majesty for YHWH or could refer to divine beings) — the encounter with the sacred as the source of discernment. Wisdom is not autonomous human cleverness but participation in the divine life through reverent knowing.
11: "For through me your days will multiply and years will be added to your life" — long life is the covenantal blessing (Deuteronomy 5:16; Proverbs 6:2; Exodus 20:12) promised to those who walk in wisdom and obedience. yarbû lāk yāmêkā (your days will be multiplied) — the promise is quantitative: more days, more years. In the ancient world where life expectancy was dramatically shorter than the modern era and early death was a common experience, the promise of extended life was genuinely significant. Wisdom is not merely qualitative improvement but literal survival advantage — the prudent person navigates dangers that kill the foolish.
12: "If you are wise, the benefit is yours alone; if you scoff, you alone bear the consequences" — wisdom and its opposite are presented as non-transferable: wisdom's benefit accrues to the individual who acquires it; the scoffer's ruin falls on himself. This is the wisdom tradition's doctrine of individual responsibility — not collective punishment but personal consequence. "You alone bear the consequences" (ûbəlaṣṭôt labbadekā tiśśāʾ) — nāśāʾ (to bear, carry) — the scoffer will carry his own penalty alone. The individualism of the wisdom tradition stands in productive tension with the communal solidarity of the prophetic tradition.
13: "Dame Folly is loud; she is naive and knows nothing" — ʾešet kəsîlût (the woman of foolishness/Folly) is the structural counterpart to Woman Wisdom of chapters 8–9. She is described as hômiyyâ (loud, tumultuous) — the same word used of the Strange Woman in Proverbs 7:11. She is pétîyyâ (naive) — using the word for the audience she targets (Proverbs 9:4, 16), as if she is only marginally more advanced than those she seduces. "She knows nothing" — biltî yādəʿâ māh (without knowing what) — her ignorance is total, making her teaching the organized transmission of nothingness.
14: "She sits at the door of her house, in a seat at the high places of the city" — Folly does not descend from her elevated position (the high places, mərōmê qāret) to the streets; she is stationary, waiting. The contrast with Wisdom's active movement (going out from her house, sending servants, climbing heights to call) is significant: Folly is passive and opportunistic, not energetically seeking. She occupies the "high places" (bāmôt) — the word also used for the illicit worship sites of Canaanite religion (1 Kings 12:31; 2 Kings 17:9), subtly aligning Folly's invitation with idolatrous practice.
15: "Calling out to those who are passing by, going straight on their way" — the people she targets are those məyaššərîm ʾōrəḥôtām (making straight their paths, going directly on their way). They are not drifting or seeking her; they are on course. Folly's strategy is interruption and distraction of the purposeful traveler. The Strange Woman of chapter 7 also targeted someone cutting through the street near her corner (Proverbs 7:8); both dangerous figures exploit the ordinary traffic of those going about their business. The danger of folly is not only for the actively searching but for the simply passing-through.
16: "Let the simple come in here" — the invitation is verbatim from Wisdom's invitation (Proverbs 9:4); the identical words from both callers set up the critical comparison. The paralleling of Wisdom and Folly through identical invitation-language makes the choice between them a matter of discernment, not obviously different appeal. To the naive, the voices may sound the same; only those already formed in wisdom can distinguish the source. This is the prologue's final pedagogical move: the formation of the student across chapters 1–9 has been precisely the cultivation of the capacity to distinguish Wisdom from Folly when their words sound alike.
17: "Stolen water is sweet, and food eaten in secret is delicious" — Folly's offer is the appeal of transgression as pleasure. mayim gənûbîm (stolen waters) and leḥem sətārîm (hidden/secret bread) — the pleasure of illicit acquisition adds flavor that lawful goods apparently lack. The psychological insight is acute: prohibition increases desire; secrecy adds excitement. The same dynamic appears in the garden temptation (Genesis 3:6, "the tree was desirable"), which Folly's invitation deliberately echoes. The offer of the forbidden as the truly satisfying is the archetype of the deceptive invitation in the biblical tradition.
18: "But he does not know that the dead are there — that her guests are in the depths of Sheol" — the structural parallel with Proverbs 7:23 (the young man "does not know that his life is the price") closes the prologue with the identical motif: unknowing. Folly's house, like the Strange Woman's, leads to Sheol. Her guests are rəpāʾîm (the shades, the weakened dead who inhabit the underworld; cf. Isaiah 26:14; Job 26:5; Psalms 88:11) — those who have already fallen and now dwell in death's depths. The prologue ends with the fate of Folly's guests starkly visible to the reader, even as it remains invisible to the naive person walking through Folly's door. The entire nine-chapter prologue has been training the reader to see what the naive cannot.
